title: Forkify Capstone Retrospective

learningObjectives.text: Explain architecture review using a new example and justify the result.
Choose and justify trade-offs using a new example and justify the result.
Explain what to carry into future projects using a new example and justify the result.
learningObjectives.subtopic: What to carry into future projects

whyThisMatters: This topic turns forkify capstone retrospective from isolated terminology into a practical tool for reading, writing, and reviewing code. It belongs to the **Application Architecture: Forkify** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Architecture review** → **Trade-offs** → **What to carry into future projects**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Architecture review
sections.body: A retrospective compares outcomes with goals, identifies successful decisions and accidental complexity, and records lessons that should influence the next project.

sections.title: Trade-offs
sections.body: Evaluate alternatives by complexity, clarity, testability, performance, team understanding, and likely change rather than searching for a universally perfect pattern.

sections.title: What to carry into future projects
sections.body: A retrospective compares outcomes with goals, identifies successful decisions and accidental complexity, and records lessons that should influence the next project.

commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.
summary: Forkify Capstone Retrospective connects Architecture review, Trade-offs, What to carry into future projects.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains “Architecture review” in the context of Forkify Capstone Retrospective?

choices.label: A variable environment records bindings for an execution context. Declaration kinds differ in scope, initialization, and observable behavior before their declaration line.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Architecture review.

choices.label: Turn a problem into observable inputs, outputs, constraints, and smaller transformations. Describe behavior before syntax, then identify the smallest step that can be tested independently.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Architecture review.

choices.label: Class syntax provides constructors, methods, static methods, extends, and super while still using prototype delegation underneath.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Architecture review.

choices.label: A retrospective compares outcomes with goals, identifies successful decisions and accidental complexity, and records lessons that should influence the next project.
choices.feedback: Architecture review is best understood as follows: A retrospective compares outcomes with goals, identifies successful decisions and accidental complexity, and records lessons that should influence the next project.

questions.prompt: A learner must use Forkify Capstone Retrospective in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Forkify Capstone Retrospective.

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Forkify Capstone Retrospective.

choices.label: Apply Architecture review deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Forkify Capstone Retrospective.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

questions.prompt: Which mental model is most useful when reasoning about Forkify Capstone Retrospective?

choices.label: The topic is best learned as a list of unrelated syntax rules.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Forkify Capstone Retrospective.

choices.label: Draw a decision map: **Architecture review** → **Trade-offs** → **What to carry into future projects**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.
choices.feedback: The correct choice connects the example to the underlying model rather than treating the syntax as isolated trivia.

choices.label: The topic matters only for naming style and does not affect behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Forkify Capstone Retrospective.

choices.label: The topic eliminates the need to model state, data flow, or dependencies.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Forkify Capstone Retrospective.

questions.explanation: The correct choice connects the example to the underlying model rather than treating the syntax as isolated trivia.
questions.hint: Trace data, control flow, ownership, or rendering step by step.

questions.prompt: Which response best addresses this common mistake: “Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.”?

choices.label: The statement is unrelated to program behavior and cannot cause defects.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Forkify Capstone Retrospective.

choices.label: The statement is correct only when variable names are short.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Forkify Capstone Retrospective.

choices.label: The statement is a misconception. A better approach is to reason from the concept's inputs, rules, and observable outcome, then verify the result.
choices.feedback: Good feedback names the misconception and replaces it with a usable reasoning process.

choices.label: The statement is always correct, so no further reasoning about Forkify Capstone Retrospective is needed.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Forkify Capstone Retrospective.

questions.explanation: Good feedback names the misconception and replaces it with a usable reasoning process.
questions.hint: Look for the option that corrects both the belief and the learner's next action.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Forkify Capstone Retrospective?

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Forkify Capstone Retrospective.

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Forkify Capstone Retrospective.

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Architecture review, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Forkify Capstone Retrospective.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Forkify Capstone Retrospective?

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Forkify Capstone Retrospective.

choices.label: Avoid Forkify Capstone Retrospective completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Forkify Capstone Retrospective.

choices.label: Use Forkify Capstone Retrospective when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

choices.label: Use Forkify Capstone Retrospective in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Forkify Capstone Retrospective.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
